title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 15, 2026
headline: Eritrea's first individual review confirms its zero score reflects real conduct, not a placeholder default.
summary: Eritrea received its first individual review tonight, after sitting at zero out of 100 for months as an unreviewed placeholder score. The review lands on the same zero, because the United Nations says Eritrea's government is still committing crimes against humanity, including indefinite forced military service that amounts to slavery. El Salvador holds at 15 of 100: a constitutional change letting the president serve indefinitely, and the exile of the country's leading human-rights group, already lowered the score nine days ago, and tonight's evidence confirms that pattern rather than adding to it. Iran holds at 2.5 of 100 as a fifth day of US-Iran fighting unfolds in the Strait of Hormuz; that fighting is scored against the United States, not Iran. Sudan, Israel, Russia, and Yemen all remain at zero, the lowest possible score, as fresh harm keeps arriving in each country. An Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo passed 719 deaths, one of the fastest-growing outbreaks on record, though the country's near-zero score cannot register the increase. Meta Platforms and xAI, maker of the Grok chatbot, both hold near the floor as a $1.4 trillion youth-safety penalty demand and an expanded deepfake lawsuit both widen without a court ruling yet. Cuba's power grid collapsed for a third time in nine days, compounded by a US oil blockade. Tunisia holds at 23.8 of 100, confirmed again by the same mass-sentencing record that produced last week's downgrade. No new score changes were proposed tonight, and no proposals are awaiting action.

pipeline.queueHandling: No proposals are awaiting action tonight. Tunisia's proposed downgrade (34.4 to 23.8 of 100) was approved and applied on July 14; no new proposals were generated this cycle, the fourth consecutive night without one.

topSignals.title: Eritrea's First Individual Review Confirms Its Zero Score Reflects Real Conduct
topSignals.whyItMatters: Eritrea's score came from a bulk data import with no individual check. Its first real review lands on the same zero of 100, because the underlying conduct still supports it.
topSignals.description: Eritrea holds a published score of 0 out of 100, the lowest score the benchmark gives. Until now, that score came from a bulk data import, not an individual review; the record showed no assessment had ever been completed. Tonight, Eritrea received its first individual review, and it lands on the same score. A United Nations human rights body has found reasonable grounds to believe Eritrea's government continues to commit crimes against humanity, including treating decades-long, open-ended forced military service as a form of slavery and making people disappear without a trace. For a third year running, Eritrea ranked last out of 180 countries on Reporters Without Borders' press freedom index. Eritrea has held no election since 1993. Separately, several July 2026 risk assessments found a sharply higher chance of renewed war between Eritrea and Ethiopia over access to Red Sea ports. That risk has not turned into a dated event, and it involves another country's actions, so the benchmark does not count it against Eritrea's own score. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: A 2016 UN inquiry found Eritrea's government and ruling party committed crimes against humanity, including enslavement and enforced disappearance, and a UN Special Rapporteur found in 2025 that reasonable grounds exist to believe these crimes continue.
Eritrea ranked last out of 180 countries in the 2026 World Press Freedom Index, for the third consecutive year.

topSignals.title: El Salvador Holds at 15 as a Downward Watch Sharpens on Two Fronts
topSignals.whyItMatters: A constitutional change letting the president serve indefinitely, and the exile of the country's top human-rights group, already cost El Salvador points nine days ago. Tonight confirms, but does not deepen, that pattern.
topSignals.description: El Salvador holds a published score of 15 out of 100, in the Critical band. Nine days ago, the benchmark lowered El Salvador's score after a constitutional amendment removed presidential term limits and Cristosal, the country's leading human-rights group, closed its offices and went into exile. More than 140 human-rights defenders and journalists fled the country between May and September. Anti-corruption official Ruth Lopez remains imprisoned. Tonight's evidence is the same amendment, the same exile, and the same imprisoned official, so it confirms that pattern rather than adding a new one. The score holds at 15 out of 100, with a strong downward watch in place for any new, discrete crackdown. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: El Salvador's Legislative Assembly amended the constitution to remove presidential term limits; the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights called it a serious setback for democracy and the rule of law.
Cristosal, El Salvador's leading human rights organization, closed its offices citing escalating repression, and at least 140 defenders and journalists fled the country.

topSignals.title: Iran's Near-Zero Score Cannot Fall Further as a Fifth Day of US-Iran Strikes Unfolds
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran's execution rate keeps climbing, but the score sits so close to zero that even a move to the floor would not count as a change under the benchmark's own math.
topSignals.description: Iran holds a published score of 2.5 out of 100, in the Critical band. Executions in Iran have passed 784 so far this year, one of the highest rates in the world, continuing a pattern already counted against the score. Only 2.5 points remain between Iran's score and zero, the lowest possible mark, so even a move all the way to the floor falls short of the five-point shift the benchmark requires before changing a published score. Separately, the ceasefire in the Strait of Hormuz fully collapsed this week. The United States reimposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports on July 14 and launched new strikes; Iran fired back at US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan. Shipping through the strait, which normally carries about a fifth of the world's energy supplies, fell to as few as 21 ships in a single day. That fighting is scored against the United States' own conduct, not against Iran, because it is one country's military action against another. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: The US reimposed its naval blockade of Iranian ports and launched a further wave of strikes; Iran retaliated with strikes on US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan, as Hormuz shipping fell to as few as 21 vessels in a day.

topSignals.title: Sudan's Genocide Finding Is Reinforced as a Similar Pattern Forms Around a Second City
topSignals.whyItMatters: The UN's gravest possible finding on mass violence still applies to Sudan, and a similar pattern is now forming in a second city, but the score was already at zero, the lowest the benchmark gives.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds a published score of 0 out of 100, the lowest score in the benchmark's Critical band. A UN Human Rights Council fact-finding mission formally found in early July that mass killings, abductions, and gang rape by the Rapid Support Forces in the city of El Fasher bear 'distinct markers of genocide.' Following a July 6 Council resolution, the mission has now opened a formal inquiry into a strikingly similar pattern forming around the city of El Obeid: encirclement, attacks on infrastructure, and blocked access to food, water, and medicine. The Rapid Support Forces are a non-state armed group, so this specific finding is attributed to their own conduct. Sudan's zero score independently reflects the Sudanese state's own record, including obstruction of humanitarian access during the same civil war. Because the score is already at the lowest possible mark, no evidence, however grave, can lower it further. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: A UN Fact-Finding Mission formally characterized RSF mass killings, abductions, and gang rape in El Fasher as bearing markers of genocide.

Following a Human Rights Council resolution, the Fact-Finding Mission opened an urgent inquiry into RSF encirclement and infrastructure attacks now underway around El Obeid, a pattern it warns mirrors El Fasher's before the city fell.

topSignals.title: Israel, Russia, and Yemen All Hold at Zero as Fresh Harm Keeps Arriving
topSignals.whyItMatters: All three countries already sit at the lowest possible score. Tonight's evidence cannot lower any of them further, but each event reinforces why the score sits at the floor.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. Israeli airstrikes killed roughly a dozen people in Gaza between July 13 and 15, including a woman and six police officers in a strike on a police post in the Jabaliya refugee camp. Gaza's Ministry of Health now attributes 1,108 people killed since the ceasefire began in October 2025. Russia holds at 0 of 100. The United Nations recorded 293 Ukrainian civilians killed and 1,990 injured in June 2026 alone, a new monthly record, driven by Russian long-range strikes on cities. Yemen holds at 0 of 100. Seventy-three United Nations staff remain arbitrarily detained by Houthi authorities, blocking aid across areas covering about 70 percent of the country's humanitarian need, while more than half the population goes hungry. None of the three scores can move lower, since all three are already at the lowest possible mark. Confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: Israeli airstrikes killed roughly a dozen people in Gaza over the two days to July 15, including six police officers in a strike on a police station in the Jabaliya refugee camp; the cumulative toll since the October 2025 truce now stands at 1,108 killed.
The UN recorded 293 civilians killed and 1,990 injured across Ukraine in June 2026, a new monthly record.

topSignals.title: An Ebola Outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo Passes 719 Deaths
topSignals.whyItMatters: One of the fastest-growing Ebola outbreaks on record keeps climbing, but the country's score is already so close to zero that the increase cannot register as a change.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds a published score of 2.3 out of 100, in the Critical band. Its Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak reached 719 confirmed deaths and 1,963 confirmed cases as of July 13, up from 600 deaths and 1,759 cases four days earlier. Health officials describe it as the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record. A clinical trial testing the antibody drug MBP134 alongside the antiviral remdesivir began July 2. The benchmark treats the epidemic itself as a natural event, not government conduct, while already-priced weaknesses in the country's health-system response sit near the floor. Because the score sits only 2.3 points above zero, no evidence this severe can register as the five-point change the benchmark requires. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: The DRC's Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak reached 719 confirmed deaths and 1,963 confirmed cases as of July 13, up from 600 deaths four days earlier, described as the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak on record.

topSignals.title: Meta and xAI Both Hold Near the Floor as Youth-Safety and Deepfake Cases Widen
topSignals.whyItMatters: A $1.4 trillion penalty demand and an expanded deepfake lawsuit are both serious, but neither is a court ruling yet, and both companies' scores already priced in conduct like this.
topSignals.description: Meta Platforms holds a published score of 7.8 out of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 ranking. A July 7 court filing revealed that state attorneys general are seeking up to $1.4 trillion in penalties in a consolidated youth-safety lawsuit, ahead of an August 18 trial in Oakland, California. That figure is a demand made by plaintiffs, not a finding by a court, and the underlying conduct is already reflected in Meta's near-floor score. xAI, maker of the Grok chatbot, holds at 0 of 100. A deepfake child-sexual-abuse-material lawsuit against xAI was amended on July 7 to add two new plaintiffs and to name Stability AI as a second defendant, alleging systemic failures to report the material to the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children. The amended filing reinforces, but cannot lower, a score already at the floor. Confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: State attorneys general are seeking up to $1.4 trillion in penalties from Meta over youth-safety claims ahead of an August 18 trial in Oakland.
An amended complaint against xAI added two new plaintiffs and named Stability AI as a co-defendant, alleging systemic failures to report Grok-generated child-sexual-abuse material to NCMEC.

topSignals.title: Cuba's Power Grid Collapses for a Third Time in Nine Days
topSignals.whyItMatters: A US oil blockade tied to last year's Venezuela intervention is compounding Cuba's aging power grid, but the country's score holds because the crisis is not, on its own, new government misconduct.
topSignals.description: Cuba holds a published score of 32.8 out of 100, in the Developing band. Cuba's national power grid collapsed on July 14 for the third time in nine days, the fifth nationwide blackout of 2026, leaving many areas without power for 20 to 22 hours a day. A United States oil blockade, imposed after the 2026 US intervention in Venezuela cut off Venezuelan crude, has left Cuba's fuel reserves exhausted. Scattered protests broke out in central Havana over the blackouts. The benchmark treats the externally-imposed blockade and Cuba's aging infrastructure as the proximate drivers, not a new act of government misconduct. Cuba's low scores for accountability and integrity already reflect its record on suppressing dissent, and no new mass-repression event was found this cycle. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Cuba's national electrical grid collapsed on July 14 for the third time in nine days, the fifth nationwide blackout of 2026, as a US oil blockade left fuel reserves exhausted.

topSignals.title: Tunisia Holds at 23.8 as a Mass Sentencing Sustains Last Week's Downgrade
topSignals.whyItMatters: A downgrade applied one day earlier already priced in this same crackdown, so tonight's evidence confirms the new score rather than pushing it lower again.
topSignals.description: Tunisia holds a published score of 23.8 out of 100, in the Developing band, after the benchmark applied a downgrade from 34.4 on July 14. The move followed a July 7-8 mass sentencing of 21 opposition figures, including Ennahda party leader Rached Ghannouchi, who received 14 years in absentia. In July, more than 400 former heads of state and human-rights leaders called for Ghannouchi's release, citing a serious decline in his health. Tonight's evidence is the same sentencing record that produced the July 14 downgrade, not a new escalation beyond it, so the score holds at 23.8. Confidence: high.

evidence.quote: Former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine sentenced to 25 years in prison
evidence.claim: A Tunis court sentenced 21 opposition figures, including Ennahda leader Rached Ghannouchi, to 12 to 35 years, sustaining the crackdown pattern behind last week's downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Tonight's evidence -- the same term-limit amendment and Cristosal's exile -- confirmed the existing pattern rather than adding a new escalation.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight; the heightened watch set July 12 remains in place.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Continued Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon remain the dominant source of harm, not Lebanon's own conduct; no new dated escalation this cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. A Balochistan security operation now reports a combined 125 alleged militants killed as of July 14, atop the already-priced enforced-disappearance pattern; the figure remains security-source-reported and unverified.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. The earthquake death toll rose to 4,333 as of July 11, a jump that reflects the disaster's scale rather than a new government failure; the evidenced decline remains short of the 5-point threshold.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. The Ebola outbreak reached 719 deaths as of July 13, up from 600 four days earlier.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. A fifth consecutive day of US-Iran strikes in the Strait of Hormuz is logged as United States conduct, not scored against Iran.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. Not assessed tonight. The calibration question against Burkina Faso has now been open roughly twenty-two consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight. The calibration question against Mali remains open roughly twenty-two consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Burkina Faso and Somalia remains open.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. Its power grid collapsed for a third time in nine days on July 14; the crisis is compounded by the US oil blockade, not a new act of Cuban government misconduct.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. A July 7 filing revealed a $1.4 trillion penalty demand ahead of the August 18 Oakland trial; the demand is not yet an adjudicated finding.

emergingRisks.risk: Eritrea's first individual review landed exactly on its existing floor score, but a sharply elevated risk of renewed Ethiopia-Eritrea war over Red Sea port access is not yet reflected in either country's own conduct score.
emergingRisks.detail: Multiple July 2026 risk assessments find a sharply higher chance of conflict, driven substantially by Ethiopian ambitions for Red Sea port access. Any dated cross-border incident would be scored as interstate conduct, not against Eritrea's internal record.

emergingRisks.risk: El Salvador's downward watch continues to sharpen as the indefinite re-election amendment and Cristosal's exile stay fully priced into a 15.0 score, with the next scheduled test not until the 2027 election.
emergingRisks.detail: A discrete new escalation, such as mass fresh detentions or formal criminalization of remaining civil society, is the trigger that would move the score again before then.

emergingRisks.risk: Iran's near-zero score leaves no room to register further deterioration even as a fifth consecutive day of US-Iran strikes unfolds in the Strait of Hormuz.
emergingRisks.detail: The blockade and strikes are US-attributable conduct, logged for the record but not scored against Iran; the United States' own Critical-band composite was not reassessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring-consistency question is now open roughly twenty-two consecutive days, the benchmark's longest-running unresolved item.
emergingRisks.detail: Neither country was assessed tonight; the underlying calibration question about the two countries' government-conduct scores remains open with no resolution timeline set.

emergingRisks.risk: The Bolivia critical-band calibration question remains open alongside Mali-Burkina Faso, with no coordinator-level review date set.
emergingRisks.detail: The question is whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions, such as state collapse or mass atrocity.

emergingRisks.risk: A cluster of zero and near-zero scores across seven countries and one AI lab means severe new evidence in any of them can no longer register as a public score change.
emergingRisks.detail: Eight of tonight's fifteen assessed entities are at or within roughly 2.5 points of the absolute floor, illustrating how the benchmark's near-floor arithmetic can outpace even severe escalations, as tonight's Sudan and DRC findings both show.

sectorAlerts.sector: Horn of Africa -- A First Review Lands on an Existing Floor, and a Genocide Finding Is Reinforced
sectorAlerts.headline: Eritrea's first individual review confirms its zero score, while Sudan's genocide finding is reinforced by a new inquiry into a second city.
observations: Eritrea (0 of 100): its first-ever individual review lands on the existing floor, based on UN findings of ongoing crimes against humanity and last-place press freedom for a third year.
Sudan (0 of 100): a new UN inquiry opens into a siege pattern forming around El Obeid, similar to the one that preceded El Fasher's fall.

An elevated risk of renewed Ethiopia-Eritrea war over Red Sea port access is logged for the record but not scored against Eritrea, since it is a risk, not a realized event, and involves another country's actions.

sectorAlerts.sector: North Africa -- A Downgrade Applied Last Week Is Sustained, Not Deepened
sectorAlerts.headline: Tunisia's score holds at 23.8 as the same mass-sentencing record that produced last week's downgrade is confirmed again.
observations: Tunisia (23.8 of 100): a July 7-8 mass sentencing of 21 opposition figures, including 14 years in absentia for Ennahda leader Rached Ghannouchi, remains the operative record.
More than 400 former heads of state and rights leaders called for Ghannouchi's release this month, citing his declining health.
The evidence sustains, rather than adds to, the downgrade applied July 14; re-proposing tonight would double-count the same conduct.

sectorAlerts.sector: Middle East -- A Fifth Day of US-Iran Strikes Unfolds as Three Countries Hold at the Floor
sectorAlerts.headline: Iran's near-zero score cannot fall further as US-Iran fighting enters a fifth day; Israel, Yemen, and Lebanon each confirm at or near the floor.
observations: Iran (2.5 of 100): executions pass 784 this year, but a five-point score change is arithmetically impossible this close to zero.
The US reimposed a naval blockade at the Strait of Hormuz and struck Iranian targets; Iran retaliated on US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan -- conduct scored against the United States, not Iran.
Israel (0 of 100) and Yemen (0 of 100) both hold at the floor as fresh harm continues to arrive in each.
Lebanon (17.2 of 100): continued Israeli strikes in the south remain the dominant source of harm, not Lebanon's own conduct.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America and the Caribbean -- A Blockade and an Earthquake Both Compound Existing Pressure
sectorAlerts.headline: Cuba's power grid collapses for a third time in nine days as Venezuela's earthquake toll and El Salvador's crackdown record both hold steady.
observations: Cuba (32.8 of 100): a US oil blockade tied to the 2026 Venezuela intervention compounds aging infrastructure; the crisis is not, on its own, new government misconduct.
Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the earthquake toll rose to 4,333, three weeks after the quake, reflecting disaster scale rather than a new government failure.
El Salvador (15.0 of 100): the same indefinite re-election amendment and Cristosal's exile that triggered a downgrade nine days ago remain the operative record.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sahel -- A Scoring-Consistency Question Passes Three Weeks Open
sectorAlerts.headline: Neither Mali nor Burkina Faso was assessed tonight, leaving their scoring-consistency question unresolved at roughly twenty-two days.
observations: Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) were both carried forward unassessed.
The question of whether the two countries are scored consistently for comparable government conduct remains open, with no resolution timeline set.

sectorAlerts.sector: Sub-Saharan Africa -- An Epidemic Passes 719 Deaths
sectorAlerts.headline: The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola outbreak reaches 719 deaths, one of the fastest-growing on record.
observations: DRC (2.3 of 100): the Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak reached 719 deaths as of July 13, up from 600 four days earlier.
A clinical trial testing the antibody drug MBP134 alongside remdesivir began July 2.

sectorAlerts.sector: Eastern Europe -- A New Monthly Casualty Record
sectorAlerts.headline: Russia holds at zero as the UN records 293 Ukrainian civilians killed in June, a new monthly record.
observations: Russia (0 of 100): the UN's June casualty count is the highest single-month total yet recorded in the conflict, driven by long-range strikes on Ukrainian cities.

sectorAlerts.sector: AI Labs -- Two Companies Hold Near the Floor as Litigation Widens
sectorAlerts.headline: Meta and xAI both hold near the floor as a youth-safety penalty demand and a deepfake lawsuit both widen without a court ruling.
observations: Meta Platforms (7.8 of 100): state attorneys general are seeking up to $1.4 trillion in penalties ahead of an August 18 trial; the figure is a demand, not an adjudicated finding.
xAI/Grok (0 of 100): an amended deepfake-CSAM lawsuit adds Stability AI as a co-defendant and alleges systemic failures to report the material to NCMEC.
Anthropic (59.1 of 100, not assessed tonight): the July 12 China-backdoor allegation remains held, with the EU AI Act becoming fully applicable August 2.

methodologyNotes.name: A First Individual Review Can Confirm a Placeholder Score Without Changing It
methodologyNotes.description: When an entity's score has never been individually reviewed -- only carried over from a bulk data import -- its first real review can land above, below, or exactly on the placeholder value. Tunisia's first review, days ago, moved its score down by 10.6 points. Eritrea's first review tonight landed on the same score, because the evidence directly supports the existing floor. Both outcomes are treated the same way: as the record's first evidence-based baseline, not as a score change requiring a proposal.

methodologyNotes.name: A Grave Finding Can Reinforce a Floor Without Changing It
methodologyNotes.description: When new evidence of extreme severity arrives for an entity already at the benchmark's lowest possible score, the score cannot move lower -- there is no room left below zero. Sudan's new UN inquiry into a siege pattern forming around El Obeid reinforces its already-floored score rather than producing a numeric change.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Near Zero Has Little Room Left to Fall
methodologyNotes.description: Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 and the Democratic Republic of the Congo's 2.3 are both so close to the lowest possible mark that even severe new evidence cannot produce the five-point change the benchmark requires before adjusting a published score. The benchmark records these as near-floor confirmations rather than treating the arithmetic limit as evidence that the underlying conduct does not matter.

methodologyNotes.name: Interstate Harm Is Scored Against the Actor, Not the Target
methodologyNotes.description: When one country's military acts against another country, the benchmark scores that action against the acting country's own conduct, not against the country it targets. The resumed US naval blockade of Iranian ports is logged as United States conduct, not scored against Iran; the elevated Ethiopia-Eritrea war risk is logged for the record but not scored against Eritrea.

methodologyNotes.name: A Legal Demand Is Not a Court Finding
methodologyNotes.description: When plaintiffs or state attorneys general ask a court for a specific penalty, that request is a demand, not a ruling. Meta's disclosure that state attorneys general are seeking $1.4 trillion in a youth-safety trial is treated as a demand under review, not as an adjudicated finding that could move the score on its own.

methodologyNotes.name: A Natural Disaster's Toll Is Not, by Itself, New Misconduct
methodologyNotes.description: When a death toll from an earthquake, flood, or epidemic keeps rising, the benchmark separates harm caused by the disaster's scale from harm caused by a government's own failure to respond. Venezuela's earthquake toll and the DRC's Ebola outbreak both grew this cycle with no documented own-conduct failure, so both scores held.

forwardTriggers.trigger: The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The Oakland youth-safety trial is the next event that could turn the $1.4 trillion penalty demand into an adjudicated finding.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.
The outcome of the UN Fact-Finding Mission's inquiry into the emerging El Obeid siege pattern is the next event to watch.
Independent confirmation of any dated Ethiopia-Eritrea border incident would count as interstate conduct, not against Eritrea's own record.
Release of detained rights defenders, or a ruling on the international clemency appeal for Rached Ghannouchi, would be the next event that could move the score back up.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Roughly twenty-two days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
Coordinator-level review of whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions. No review date has been set.
A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
First confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
Independent verification of any renewed attempt at the collapsed prisoner exchange is the next event that would allow a scored assessment.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Tunisia's bulk-import placeholder fell 10.6 points on its first individual review, while Eritrea's landed unchanged at zero; does this first-assessment protocol treat placeholder conversions consistently, or does the outcome hold only when evidence happens to match the default?
